meta.title: Comparisons as predictable as the sunrise
meta.description: An analysis of 200,000 similes from popular fiction.

content.value: Comparisons as predictable as the sunrise
An analysis of 200,000 similes from popular fiction.
By Russell Samora. Design by Shelly Tan.
I pulled every simile in the form “as ___ as ___” from tens of thousands of books for the top 500 most common adjectives.
Maybe you haven’t considered the simile since grade school, so here is a pop-quiz to get you in a writer’s mindset: Fill in the blank.
Above are real results from my extensive analysis of this specific form of simile. Once you start looking, you see them everywhere, from the classics Jane Eyre to last year’s darling Heart the Lover.

items.quote: Remain where you are till I return; be as still as a mouse.
items.title: Jane Eyre

items.quote: I’m like my mother, I’m as useless as a beggar in Calcutta.
items.title: Heart the Lover

items.quote: I had part of a cup of coffee and about half of some cake that was as hard as a rock.
items.title: The Catcher in the Rye

items.quote: My mouth has gone as dry as sawdust.
items.title: The Hunger Games

items.quote: And Sunny remembered Ulla, who was so nonchalant about such matters that it made negotiations around sex as natural as a conversation between children playing in a sandbox.
items.title: The Loneliness of Sonia and Sunny

items.quote: It offered twenty-one guest rooms, commanding sea views, and a lobby fireplace as big as a truck.
items.title: All the Light we Cannot See

items.quote: Sparrows, awake even at night, chittered in the rafters, high above men who sat with meager scatterings of field mushrooms and pond greens, buckets whose bottoms were populated with shrimp as slender as pins, and snails collected from village porches.
items.title: A Guardian and a Thief

content.value: I thought it would be a trivial exercise, but the more I poked around, the more questions I had. Let’s explore what I learned.

value.content: English has lots of ways to make comparisons. “Eyes like daggers,” “razor-sharp wit.” Most of these figurative forms are difficult to extract from text at scale. “As ___ as ___” is the exception because of its rigid structure. This makes it the most reliable to parse programmatically while minimizing the need for human judgement. It’s also surprisingly common in all forms of fiction. So while it’s just a sample of figurative language, it provides a quantifiable glimpse into the topic.

content.value: Every adjective has a fingerprint
So that we’re on the same page, here is a diagram of this form of simile:

First, a notable disclaimer. We’ll mostly just look at a simplified version of this form; grounds that are adjectives, and vehicles that are nouns (specifically mono/bi-grams, i.e., one or two words). This reduces the noise in favor of a clearer signal. For example:
“In the doorway stood Kristen, Dylan, and Alicia, looking as stunned as Taylor Swift during the 2009 VMAs when Kanye stole her acceptance speech.”
These one-offs can be fun and evocative if you know the context, but aren’t helpful when looking for data trends.

In similes, every adjective has a distinct shape. If you look at the usage of the nouns that follow, you can see whether an adjective is dominated by a single cliché or has range. Here is the shape of dry from our pop quiz.

You’ll notice that the top 3–bone, desert, and dust–make up 43% of all usage, and there is a pretty quick drop-off after that with a long tail of smaller ones. Let’s zoom out to see what the shape of more adjectives.
Each tiny chart is one adjective’s fingerprint. Most have a similar skewed distribution, with some key distinctions:

li: A few have a gentler slope which tells us there is no real cultural default.
Most are dominated by a few go-to nouns then have a long tail.
The clichés are marked by a single tall spike and virtually nothing else surfacing.

content.value: <mark>walk through an example or two? or just click to enlarge</mark>

Most similes follow a similar shape, probably due to a blend of factors: cultural connections, literary precedent, and perhaps sometimes plain laziness. Some writers reach for a novel comparison to make it their own, while others just want one that’s reliable and accessible.
todo - brief note on it still being used in popular fiction from this year.
Specialist and generalists

Now let’s flip it around and look at it from the noun’s perspective. Some are wielded as a comparison for dozens of different adjectives (generalists), while some are uniquely tied to a single quality (specialists).
As cool as a cucumber is the paragon specialist. Cucumber (or as I learned that it sometimes was referred to as “cowcumber”) doesn’t even crack the top ten for any other adjective.
On the other hand, you have hell which is the top noun for nineteen different adjectives.

value.description: If you pick two similes for a noun at random, what is the percentage chance the adjective matches?

content.value: Most nouns land somewhere in between. They have some reliable pairings, sometimes because of distinct qualities (e.g., glass is smooth, fragile, transparent) or amplified because of similar adjectives (e.g., the sun is bright, brilliant, radiant).
But like most things, the interesting stuff lies at the extremes–nouns so tightly coupled with a single adjective they’ve become idioms, or so plamigerent that they’ve lost all meaning.
Four nouns, four stories
Let’s focus on four nouns that each reveal something different about how writers use comparisons.
Cat
Cat is a noun of contradictions. Writers use it to mean nervous and relaxed, active and lazy.
<mark>a chart showing the breadth of adjectives (can i use some quant. metric like cosine sim to make the point with data?)

While most animals are pigeon-holed–think stubborn as a mule–cats span a huge range of observed behavior, and no single adjective dominates. Cats are the number one most used noun for nine different adjectives, from agility to anxiety to stealth.
<mark>chart of cats vs other animals</mark>
Stone
Stone is the opposite. Where a cat is defined by the range of things it does, stone is often defined by what it lacks. It doesn’t move, speak, feel, or think.
<mark> chart of stone’s categories</mark>

It ranks first for 17 adjectives, but nearly all of them point in a similar direction; Hard, solid, firm. Still, motionless, immovable. Silent, mute, dumb. Blank, impassive, expressionless. It is mostly a bunch of different words that say “nothing is happening here,” which makes it a blank and malleable canvas.
Child
The adjectives paired with children are more about what adults project onto them.
<mark>chart of some buckets of child’s adjectives</mark>

The most common pairings are divided between two camps: fragility and pureness. On the fragile side: helpless, innocent, weak, ignorant, vulnerable, foolish, lost. On the pure side: excited, delighted, eager, happy, pleased.
Both say more about the adult writing the simile than the child comparison being invoked. Child is unique in its dual nature since most nouns carry just one emotional note.
Hell
Hell is one of the most versatile nouns in the dataset. It ranks first for 20 different adjectives, more than any other noun. However, most of them have nothing to do with hell.

As hot as hell makes sense, if you subscribe to the religious imagery. But sexy as hell? Confused as hell? Similes usually work because the noun possesses the quality. Hell often functions more as an amplifier.
<mark>chart showing hell’s adjectives, their size, and color-coded if they have a real connection to the word or not</mark>
That makes hell pretty unique in the dataset. Most nouns earn their place by embodying something, whereas hell just became another way to say “very.”
The funny ones

But not all similes are used as intended. Take the classic expression “as clear as mud.” It inverts the expectation by using a contrasting quality of the noun, often for humor. This becomes more of an art form as the comparison gets more specific.
“He looks about as happy as a dad at a Taylor Swift concert, but at least he’s in control of his rage.”

While these are a small subset of the dataset, a kind of counter-culture of the simile world, they often paint a more vivid and memorable picture. These tend to work best with positive-sentiment adjectives to set up a punch line.
<mark>fun graphic</mark>
Data and method
